text: Hello, my name is Dieter Pohl. I'm one of the surgeons together with Dr. Blumenthal and Dr. Christian here at Roger Williams Medical Center in the weight loss surgery program.
And today I will talk to you about the laparoscopic pancreatic diversion with duodenal switch surgery, also called in the short version duodenal switch or even shorter DS.
If you want to hear more about the surgery you can make an appointment with one of us for zero one five two one six three one zero or go to our website loseweightri.com.
The patient in this video is a forty five year old man with a body mass index of fifty.

The video shows the patient's abdomen with six marked incision sites: four are a quarter of an inch and two are half an inch. Cannulas are being introduced through these incisions for instrument access. The yellow tissue visible is fat, and the red tissue on top is muscle, as the surgical team operates in the right upper part of the body.

The surgeon identifies the grayish structure as the colon on the right side of the surgical field, ensuring adequate reach for the procedure. The surgeon moves along the right side of the body, grasping tissue with the left hand.

In the left upper corner of the laparoscopic view, the surgeon identifies the beginning of the colon, known as the right colon. The small intestines are visible, and the surgeon measures back from this point.
The surgeon uses graspers to measure the small intestine, marking every four inches. At the midpoint of five feet, a clip is placed to mark the spot.

The surgeon marks a point five feet from the beginning of the small intestine, indicating where the second part of the intestinal connection will be made. This section is visually identified as the area where food will enter and digestion of carbohydrates will occur, with absorption happening further along towards the colon.

The surgeon identifies the correct location and marks it with a stitch, suturing this part to the belly side wall for easy identification during the upcoming reattachments and realignments of the intestines.
The camera provides a view of the entire abdominal cavity, showing the placement of surgical markers and the spatial arrangement of the intestines and colon.

The surgeon inserts a liver retractor to lift the brownish liver, revealing the pinkish stomach on the right. The laparoscopic view shows limited space between the stomach and liver, approximately less than an inch. The surgeon identifies and grasps the end of the stomach, measuring back about four to five centimeters.

The surgeon identifies a spot approximately two inches in length, which will be used as a marker for the end of the stomach freeing process. The anatomical structures are visibly interconnected within the surgical field.

The surgeon detaches the pinkish organ of the stomach from surrounding structures, using a grasping tool to free it up to the diaphragm. The diaphragm is visible, with the heart and lungs located on the other side.

The entire stomach is visible, fully detached along its outer edge. The spleen is located in the right upper corner of the screen. An endoscope is introduced into the stomach, extending to its end, serving as a guide to ensure the passage is not too constricted.

The surgeon uses a stapler along the guide to position three rows of titanium staples on each side of the stomach, simultaneously cutting and sealing the tissue. A knife is visible at the top, cutting through the stomach, resulting in the separation of the sleeve stomach on the left side and the excised portion on the right.

The surgeon continues stapling and cutting along the endoscope positioned on the left side within the stomach, creating a looser sleeve compared to a standalone sleeve gastrectomy. The procedure progresses towards the diaphragm, with attention to any bleeding at the bottom of the field, which is managed with stitches or clips. If a hiatal hernia is observed, it will be repaired concurrently.

The surgeon displays the sleeve stomach on the left side, which holds two to three ounces of fluid. On the right side, the sleeve leads to the duodenum, which is being detached from surrounding fat. A tunnel is being created behind the duodenum, with the pancreas visible below the cutting instrument, which is used to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon pulls the duodenum away from the bile duct, with the liver visible above and the gallbladder on the left. A device is introduced to complete a tunnel behind the duodenum. There is minor bleeding in the area, which is controlled using stitches, staples, or clips. The duodenum is stapled approximately an inch and a half below the stomach, with visible blood on the left side, which is stopped by stitches.

The surgeon creates a hole in the duodenum using cautery, allowing for connection to the marked intestine. Yellow bile fluid is visible as it emerges from the duodenum. The surgeon examines the left side where the intestine is attached, noting difficulty in reaching due to excess fat.

The surgeon removes yellow adipose tissue obstructing the view of the colon. The previously marked section of the intestines is detached from the abdominal wall. A new incision is made in the intestines, which are then attached at a marked location approximately ten feet proximal to the colon.

The surgeon uses a grasper to hold the intestine approximately ten feet before the colon, maneuvering through a significant amount of fat. A stapler is inserted to create a wide opening in the intestine, ensuring there is no risk of narrowing or stricturing.

The surgeon closes the stapler around the duodenum, applying staples on both sides. The gallbladder is visible in the background. Upon opening the stapler, the inside of the intestines is exposed, showing bile and a large hole. The surgeon places stitches at the corners to prevent leakage and ensure proper sealing.

The surgeon places stitches in the remaining opening to secure it, followed by using a stapler to remove a small piece of tissue. The area is now visibly sealed and closed, with a wide and unobstructed opening.
The surgeon identifies the section in the middle where pancreatic juices and bile will enter, preparing to cut this part again as part of the duodenal switch procedure.

The surgeon connects the duodenum to the intestines, placing a stitch in the bottom corner to ensure proper healing and reduce tension. On the right side, a light shines through the stomach sleeve, while the left side shows the intestines occluded with a grasper. Water is applied over the area to perform a leak test.

The surgeon performs a leak test by blowing air into the area where the light is shining on the right side, observing for air bubbles underwater to confirm no leakage. Once confirmed, the surgeon proceeds to trace backwards along the intestines, which transport food from the sleeve towards the colon, until reaching the marked halfway point approximately five feet from the colon. Here, the surgeon attaches the section of intestines where digestive juices flow in.

The surgeon uses a stapler to create holes on each side of the intestinal segment that will connect to the colon, joining the two pieces together. The technique resembles that used in gastric bypass, with staples applied in both directions to prevent narrowing or kinking, ensuring a wide connection between the intestinal segments.

One of the things that can always happen with any surgery, even any general surgery, other surgery, is that a person develops scar tissue in the inside, even years later, and develops a bowel blockage from that. That's a rare occurrence, but it does happen once in a while to people, and then that person would need a surgery to repair this.

The surgeon closes a small opening with a stapler and reinforces the corners with additional stitches to prevent leaks and tension. The connection allows food to flow from the right side bottom intestine to the left side bottom intestine, then to the colon, where absorption occurs over the last five feet.
The surgeon places stitches in the intestinal area to prevent bowel blockages, addressing potential gaps between layers of fat that could lead to an internal hernia.

The surgeon places the detached stomach into a plastic bag to prevent contamination as it is removed through a half-inch incision. The surgeon uses a finger to check for air leaks before suturing the incisions to prevent hernias. The abdomen is washed, and cannulas are removed, revealing six incisions.
So again, this is our team. If you're interested in learning about the surgery, go to our website, loseweightri.com or call the office and make an appointment. Thank you.
